cataloging_subject.label: Jack and His Stepdame
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous fifteenth-century Middle English comic verse tale represented by CME00073, known across manuscript and print versions as Jack and His Stepdame and The Friar and the Boy. The cataloging subject is the medieval work rather than one immutable authorial text: surviving versions change titles and endings. This record distinguishes the work from the Rawlinson and Brogyntyn physical witnesses, Halliwell's 1855 transcription, Zupitza's 1893 edition and apparatus, the University of Michigan encoding, and the checked-in repository XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: Jack and His Stepdame
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Probably composed in the fifteenth century; attested circa 1470; CME source edition published in 1893
catalog_summary.region: England; Norfolk is the probable poet's dialect area, while the surviving witnesses have distinct copying and circulation contexts

notes: Modern preferred title used by the Middle English Texts Series and normalized from the Rawlinson title tradition.

titles.value: The Tale of Jacke and His Step Dame
notes: Zupitza reports that this wording was added at the opening of the Rawlinson copy by a later hand; spelling and capitalization are normalized for discovery.
Title of Zupitza's source edition, with I/J normalized as Jak. The XML trailer prints 'Iak and his step dame.'

Established title for the early-print and later reception tradition; Halliwell names the item this way in his contents and discussion even though the text itself begins beneath Roman numeral VII without a verbal heading.

titles.value: God, that died for vs all

notes: Final verse line of the R-based edited text, followed by Amen and the editorially reproduced closing trailer.

agents.name: Julius Zupitza
notes: Editor of the 1893 article and source text encoded as CME00073.

agents.name: James Orchard Halliwell-Phillipps
notes: Transcriber of the Brogyntyn/Porkington version through which Zupitza knew witness P.

scope.description: P variants, comparative readings, and three supplied lines mediated through Halliwell's 1855 transcription

notes: This responsibility applies to the mediated P material in the source edition, not to authorship of the medieval work.

assertion.notes: The estimate is based on the manuscript history; no authorial or authoritative original survives.

date_statements.display: Attested circa 1470 in National Library of Wales, Brogyntyn MS II.1

assertion.notes: This is the earliest securely dated extant witness verified in this research, not a claim that the poem was composed in 1470; the catalog date is approximate even though the normalized field records its central year.

places.label: Norfolk
notes: Probable area of the poet's dialect according to the modern METS analysis; not a verified manuscript production place for every witness.

assertion.notes: The conclusion combines restricted rhyme and verbal forms; scribal revision has obscured much authorial dialect evidence.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: German prose in Zupitza's notes belongs to the source-edition apparatus, not to the medieval intellectual work cataloged here.

form_statements.description: A stanzaic comic narrative; early versions use six-line, loosely accentual aa4b3cc4b3 stanzas.

genre_statements.label: Bourde
notes: Cataloged in the Middle English Texts Series collection Ten Bourdes and within its Tales of White Magic group.

genre_statements.label: Folk tale
notes: The boy's reward with a magic object belongs to ATU 592, The Dance Among Thorns.

subject_statements.label: Magic gifts and compulsions
notes: Jack receives an unerring bow, a pipe that compels hearers to dance, and a curse that makes his stepmother break wind when she looks angrily at him.

subject_statements.label: Stepmother, child mistreatment, and family conflict
Clergy, religious orders, and friars
notes: The friar is a target of anticlerical comedy in the CME version.

subject_statements.label: Compelled dancing
notes: The METS reference section identifies the relevant international tale type as ATU 592, The Dance Among Thorns. The URI is omitted at the identifier level because no authoritative public ATU record URL was verified.

summaries.value: Jack, underfed and mistreated by his stepmother, shares his poor meal with a hungry old man and receives three marvelous gifts: an unfailing bow, a pipe that forces everyone who hears it to dance, and a curse that makes his stepmother break wind whenever she glares at him. Jack's use of the gifts humiliates both his stepmother and a friar and eventually compels a family accommodation. Surviving versions differ especially in their endings; later printed forms add an ecclesiastical-court episode absent from the R-based CME text.

The CME representation follows Zupitza's R-based ending while retaining a P-only alternate final stanza inside the apparatus. Its 73 top-level stanza groups therefore describe the edited R stream, not a universal stanza count for every version of the work.

extent.description: One verse tale represented by 73 top-level LG groups and 438 top-level L elements: numbered stanzas 1–71 plus 52a and 59a. The raw XML totals of 74 LG and 450 L elements include a twelve-line P-only alternate stanza nested within the note attached to the final R stanza. The encoding also retains 17 printed-page breaks (pp. 66–82), fourteen untyped R folio milestones (52r–59r), 17 Halliwell-page milestones, 79 Hazlitt-line milestones, and 340 notes. These are edition and encoding metrics, not a normalized count for every historical version.

content_parts.label: Jack and His Stepdame
content_parts.summary: The complete R-based comic verse tale represented by CME00073, from the prayerful incipit through Jack's magical gifts, the humiliation of his stepmother and the friar, family reconciliation, and the final prayer.

locators.value: EEBO/TEXT/BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem']; 73 direct LG children and 438 direct L children

locators.scheme: comparative version folios and IIIF canvases

notes: The Brogyntyn and Halliwell locators identify the P version used for comparison, not a claim that P and R are textually identical.
The twelve lines nested in the final-stanza note are an alternate P ending and are excluded from the direct R-stream metrics.

related_works.title: The Friar and the Boy (extended early-print version)
notes: Early printed versions add an ecclesiastical-court episode after the point where R, P, and Q move to their shorter endings.

related_works.title: The Dance Among Thorns
notes: The tale type covers the reward of a boy with a magic object that compels dancing.

evidence.locator: whole XML tree and direct-child XPath counts
evidence.summary: Verifies one poem, 73 direct LG groups and 438 direct L elements, one nested twelve-line P-only alternate stanza, 17 page breaks, fourteen R folio milestones, 17 Halliwell-page milestones, 79 Hazlitt-line milestones, 340 notes, and the exact repository checksum.
Confirms the public U-M identity, item title, editor, correct 1893 source date, and electronic-text access.

evidence.citation: Julius Zupitza, 'Jak and his step dame,' Archiv 90 (1893), 57–82
evidence.summary: Primary source for the source-edition title, editor, 1893 date, R locus and direct transcription, mediated P comparison through Halliwell, stanza numbering and variants, editorial method, title wording, incipit, explicit, and text extent.

evidence.citation: J. O. Halliwell, Early English Miscellanies (London: Warton Club, 1855), 46–62
evidence.summary: Primary source for Halliwell's direct transcription of the Porkington manuscript, the comparative P text used by Zupitza, the conventional Friar and the Boy title, and exact printed work boundary.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, MS. Rawl. C. 86 catalog and complete digital object

evidence.citation: Melissa Furrow, Introduction to Jack and His Stepdame, Middle English Texts Series
evidence.locator: Manuscripts, Scribes, and Printed Texts; Reference Tools; Poet, Poetry, and Language
evidence.summary: Current scholarly evidence for preferred and alternate titles, anonymous and non-authoritative textual status, wider version history and differing endings, R and P loci, fifteenth-century composition, earliest print, NIMEV 977, ATU 592, six-line stanza form, and probable Norfolk authorial dialect.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's composition be dated more narrowly than the fifteenth century without mistaking witness dates for authorial dates?
next_steps: Review the full textual and linguistic argument across the earliest R, P, Q, and E versions.
Keep the circa-1470 Brogyntyn date separately labeled as first known attestation.

open_questions.question: Does the probable Norfolk poet's dialect justify a more specific composition-place claim?
next_steps: Compare the METS dialect argument with LALME and textual work on scribal revision.
Continue to distinguish poet's dialect, scribal dialect, manuscript production, ownership, and circulation.

open_questions.question: Is there any defensible author attribution beyond Anonymous?
next_steps: Retain anonymous authorship unless direct scholarly evidence establishes a supported attribution.
Do not infer authorship from later owners, scribes, or printers.

notes: The source edition's German apparatus is described in lineage but excluded from the abstract work's language and form summaries.
The tale includes misogynistic stepmother characterization, child mistreatment, scatological humor, and comic violence; subject labels describe historical content rather than endorse it.

A complete transmission history would include additional manuscript and print versions, including a fire-damaged Cotton witness and multiple early chapbooks. This record remains partial because those non-immediate branches have not been independently manifested.
